name: Papa Charlie Jackson
abstract: William Henry "Papa Charlie" Jackson (November 10, 1887 – May 7, 1938) was an early African American bluesman and songster who accompanied himself with a banjo guitar, a guitar, or a ukulele. His recording career began in 1924.

about_quotes.speaker: Buddy Burton
about_quotes.evidence: And so at that time Papa Charlie Jackson was going big with the, he's playing the guitar and singing Uncle Bud and all them type of blues. And so Melrose had a lot of confidence in me... He says, but he says, can't you get something together, something like Papa Charlie Jackson

about_quotes.speaker: Ralph Emerson Brown and Jasper Taylor
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] ...And Jackson, Benjo, Charlie Jackson. Because he does the Salty Dog on... [INTERVIEWER] Oh yeah. I don't know whether, I couldn't say Jackson with Freddie. Freddie was from there. Jackson didn't even need music.
about_quotes.doc_title: Ralph Emerson Brown and Jasper Taylor Interview, 1961-05-23

about_quotes.speaker: Walter "Furry" Lewis
about_quotes.evidence: I was sort of playing then, named Charlie Jackson and Landis Waldron. They was playing... And I was just a singer. And so I just started playing and singing myself.

about_quotes.speaker: Bob Shoffner
about_quotes.evidence: Then Charlie Jackson, he played bass saxophone. And Joe started experimenting with the bass saxophone. You see, and he had a, oh, I see, we were playing trombone.

about_quotes.speaker: Angola Talking
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] you say Papa Charlie Jackson yes and who else Lion Lemon [INTERVIEWEE] oh yeah Lion Lemon
